title: Neighbor accused of sexual abuse
summary: A 51-year-old city bus driver faces a 20-count criminal complaint accusing him of sexually abusing three young sisters who live in his trailer park. Police arrested Michael C. Lee last week. He is being held in Cook Inlet Pre-Trial Facility in lieu of $50,000 bail on charges of sexually abusing the girls, ages 9, 10, and 11, in his trailer on Brayton Drive. The complaint details the allegations against Lee as follows: Lee's wife, Jacklyn, called police on the morning of Feb. 2. She told an officer she came home the day before to find the 11-year-old girl naked in her bed. The girl told Detective Cindi Stanton she went to Lee's trailer after school that day. "Mike took her into his bedroom, took off her clothes, and pushed her onto the bed," the complaint states. "Mike then grabbed a rope f…

title: Upper Kenai River to open for subsistence
summary: Subsistence fishing for salmon, trout and other fish will soon be legal on the upper Kenai River and other federal waters on the Kenai Peninsula, and salmon dipnetters will share the Copper River with fish wheels. The Federal Subsistence Board made those decisions affecting popular fishing areas Tuesday as it considered a series of proposals from around the state. The board continues to meet today and Thursday at the Egan Center. The Kenai decision marks the first time since the 1950s that a freshwater subsistence fishery has been allowed on the Kenai Peninsula. The board established a subsistence fishery for salmon, trout, Dolly Varden and char on federal waters on the peninsula and west side of Cook Inlet, and opened it up to all qualified rural residents throughout the state. The Kenai …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | State assets secure with Phillips MERGER: New company is likely to remain 'bullish on Alaska.' By Ben Spiess Anchorage Daily News (Published: November 20, 2001) The proposed $35 billion merger of Phillips Petroleum Co. and Conoco Inc. will have minimal impact on the company's Alaska business, Kevin Meyers, president of Phillips Alaska Inc., said Monday. "I fully expect Alaska will remain a key asset in the combined company," Meyers said. He said the deal will not change the investments proposed for Alaska next year. Meyers also said he expects few if any layoffs among the 940 employees of Phillips Alaska. Conoco has no oil production and no employees in Alaska. If the deal is approved, some Conoco employees may come to Alaska and some Alaska employees could be transferred. "The botto…

title: Green Connection owners make a tropical move
summary: Three weeks after they met, Steve and Beth Ann Shropshire went to Hawaii together. "We made a pact we were going to come back someday and plant some roots," Steve Shropshire said. They recently made good on that promise to themselves, literally and figuratively. After 24 years of running a "plantscaping" business in Anchorage, the Shropshires have transplanted themselves and an offshoot of their business to the Big Island. In 1977, Steve Shropshire, then in his late teens, started the landscaping business The Green Connection, putting greenery into buildings from the Egan Civic and Convention Center to the Anchorage 5th Avenue mall. Beth Ann joined him after they married in 1984, and the team expanded into retail and exterior landscaping. Over the years, the couple said, they came to favor…

title: Federal court to review Tongass logging injunction impact
summary: JUNEAU -- A federal court hearing will be held this week to look at the impact of an injunction against logging in the Tongass National Forest. U.S. District Judge James Singleton last month was leaning toward issuing a narrowly-tailored injunction to protect wilderness, but decided an evidentiary hearing was needed first. The three-day hearing is scheduled to start Wednesday. At issue is whether and how the court should enjoin some timber sales in the Tongass as a court-ordered wilderness review moves forward. Last year Singleton ruled that the Forest Service violated federal law in 1997 as it updated the Tongass Land Management Plan by failing to consider some roadless area eligible for wilderness designation by Congress. The judge then issued a temporary injunction that halted logging o…

title: Woman's body found at downtown motel
summary: An unidentified woman was found dead Monday on the outdoor walkway of a closed downtown motel. Anchorage police said a passer-by saw the woman lying naked on the second-floor walkway of the Rodeway Inn, 1104 E. Fifth Ave., shortly before noon. More than three hours later, her dark hair and upper back were visible from the street through the wood planks of the railing as police photographed the scene. Late Monday afternoon, Detective Joe Hoffbeck said the woman had not been identified. There is no obvious cause of death, and Hoffbeck said an autopsy will probably be performed today. It appeared she died where she was found, Hoffbeck said. The Rodeway Inn was closed by the city about a month ago for various fire code violations and has since been vacant. Hoffbeck said police found sleeping b…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Congressional audit delays Chugach forest management plan QUESTIONS: Young asked investigators to look at process leading to revision. By Elizabeth Manning Anchorage Daily News (Published: February 11, 2002) A final revision of the Chugach National Forest management plan was due to be unveiled last month. But a congressional audit has delayed the process, and forest officials don't expect its release until at least March. Once complete, the plan will govern management of the forest for the next 10 to 15 years. It will outline which lands can be developed and which lands cannot, where snowmachines and other motor vehicles may roam and where they may not, among other issues. Like most forest plans, the one for the Chugach has been controversial. The revision has taken four years and co…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Eight Tongass timber sales in court tug of war HEARING: Judge to determine if new logging ban is needed. The Associated Press Wire (Published: February 15, 2002) Juneau -- Eight Southeast Alaska timber sales are being scrutinized in a hearing this week in federal court in Juneau. U.S. Forest Service officials, timber industry advocates, conservationists and Southeast community leaders were in court Thursday for the second of three days of testimony about a possible Tongass logging injunction. U.S. District Judge James Singleton ruled last year that the Forest Service violated federal law when it failed to consider some areas for wilderness designation when it issued its 1997 Tongass Land Management Plan. Singleton issued a two-month injunction last year and is presiding over this wee…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Public meeting aimed at Amchitka radiation victims Daily News Staff Staff (Published: January 8, 2002) If you or a family member worked in the U.S. nuclear weapons program on Amchitka Island before Jan. 7, 1974, the advisory board of a medical surveillance program invites you to a public meeting Wednesday evening in Anchorage to learn about compensation for workers made sick by radiation from the bomb tests. Men and women who worked at the site in the Aleutian Chain and other nuclear sites around the country have developed certain cancers, probably linked to radiation exposure during and after tests of nuclear warheads, said Don Weber, outreach director for the Amchitka Project. Workers who develop such cancers will be compensated, or if they are deceased, their survivors will be com…

U.S. District Judge James Singleton last month was leaning toward issuing a narrowly-tailored injunction to protect wilderness, but decided an evidentiary hearing was needed first. title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Governor's chief of staff to resign NEW JOB: Jim Ayers to head environmental firm Oceana Inc. By Ben Spiess Anchorage Daily News (Published: December 5, 2001) Jim Ayers, Gov. Tony Knowles' chief of staff, announced Tuesday he plans to resign next month to head the Alaska office of the marine environmental group Oceana Inc. Ayers is the highest ranking staff member in the Knowles administration and has served with the two-term governor from the beginning in December 1992. He will stay in the job until Jan. 4. The governor has not announced a replacement. As chief of staff, Ayers was policy manager, enforcer and traffic cop and served the longest tenure of any chief of staff in Alaska state history, said Knowles spokesman Bob King. Ayers, 55, played the role with verve. He was known fo…

title: Democrats kill Fairbanks debate
summary: Juneau -- A move to take the subsistence debate to Fairbanks, where resistance to a rural priority has traditionally been strong, was turned away by Senate Democrats on Friday. Senate Minority Leader Johnny Ellis called the plan a campaign stunt by Sen. Robin Taylor, who is running for lieutenant governor. "The whole thing has definite political and electoral overtones," said Ellis, a Democrat from Anchorage. "I think the Republicans really ought to reflect on whether that is a wise use of public funds." Taylor is a Republican from Wrangell who chairs the state Senate Judiciary Committee. On Friday he introduced a resolution urging the Legislature to file a lawsuit challenging the Alaska National Interest Lands Conservation Act. Then he sought permission from the full Senate to hold a Judi…

title: Magazine article that mentions fishing technique illegal on Kenai raises anglers' ire
summary: Two sentences in the latest issue of "Outdoor Life" magazine are causing quite a stir among Kenai River rainbow trout anglers. The February-March issue features Alaska roadside angling opportunities that some anglers fear will only add to the normal crowding on accessible streams and rivers come fishing season. But that isn't the big deal. Outdoor Life fishing editor Jerry Gibbs generates the big deal in describing how to use plastic beads, usually bought at craft stores in the sizes and colors of salmon eggs, to imitate salmon eggs to catch Kenai trout. "The bead is toothpick-pegged an inch above a size 8 or 10 octopus-style hook," he wrote. "If you peg the egg at the hook-eye, you'll get fewer strikes." Bead fishing for rainbows has long been a contentious issue in Alaska. Some true-blue…

title: Seward receives state's first 'TsunamiReady' designation
summary: If you were standing somewhere in Seward while a tsunami, or huge sea wave, were bearing down on the town, there's almost no way you wouldn't hear about it. Therefore, you would have time at least to make a move toward higher, safer ground. Seward, which just won the honor of being designated Alaska's first "TsunamiReady" community, has multiple means of getting the word out. Emergency personnel, for example, would sound a siren throughout the town, warning specifically of a coming tsunami. They also would engage a computerized phone-calling system, use the public-address speakers on their vehicles, get out the warning on AM and FM radio stations and go door-to-door if need be. "We have a lot of backup," said Chief David Squires of the Seward Fire Department. It was that backup and other m…

title: Protected land triples; species still at risk
summary: Washington -- The amount of land in North America protected from development tripled over the past three decades, yet pollution, hunting and loss of habitat still threaten at least 235 plants and animals, says a study by the United States, Canada and Mexico. Since 1970, the acreage off-limits to development rose from 247 million to 741 million -- about 15 percent of the continent's land surface. Creation of wilderness areas accounts for the increase. Those include a doubling in the size of U.S. areas in 1980 with the enactment of the Alaska National Interest Lands Act. Nineteen "biosphere reserves" were created in Mexico in the 1990s, and Canada has tripled the area of protected sites over the past three decades. But increased trade across borders raises the need for more collaboration to …

title: Who owns Alaska, all the people or a privileged few?
summary: "Who Owns America?" is the question former Secretary of the Interior Wally Hickel asked more than 30 years ago in his widely read book of the same title. The answer, according to the back of the book's jacket, is "the country's resources -- air, mineral, water and public lands -- belong to all the people, not just a special few." Maybe it's time now that someone ask "Who Owns Alaska?" If you are living under the illusion that the state Hickel governed from 1966 to 1968 and came back to govern again from 1990 to 1994 is that place that might "belong to all the people, not just a special few," think again. Look no further than the sheltered and salmon-rich waters of Chignik Bay, 500 miles southwest Anchorage on the Alaska Peninsula. By a vote of 7-0, the Alaska Board of Fisheries earlier thi…

title: Ball fields, bed tax set for hearing
summary: Confused about when your city government will deal with issues ranging from the Simonian Little League to a convention center? Think Johnny Cash. The country music legend turns 70 on Feb. 26 -- the same day the Anchorage Assembly will have public hearings on whether to increase a hotel bed tax to finance a new convention center, and for bonds for police and fire services, Eagle River parks and the city's emergency operations center. Assembly Chairman Dick Traini said he expects the Assembly to also vote on Feb. 26 whether to put those proposals on an April 2 ballot. The Assembly may also take action on the Simonian Little League that night, although it may happen some other time within the next three weeks. Mayor George Wuerch vetoed an Assembly resolution Tuesday that said he should look …

title: Transcript available over Internet
summary: Fights over political districting are as old as democracy, but the technology that allows anyone with a good computer to follow the redistricting trial now under way in Anchorage as it happens, word-for-word, is new to Alaska. It's called real-time court reporting. The Alaska Court System records all court proceedings on tape or CD. If someone wants a printed transcript, they have to arrange to have it typed from the recordings, which is a laborious process, especially if the lawyers want a record of testimony at the end of each day. Meet Sandra Mierop of Pacific Rim Reporting. She sits in the well of Judge Mark Rindner's fourth-floor courtroom, right next to the witness stand. When anyone talks, her fingers flash on the black keys of one of those little machines court reporters use. Using…

title: Artist sues police over rape case
summary: A well-known local artist says police officers who took her into protective custody while she was in an alcoholic blackout failed to notice she had been raped because they are prejudiced against intoxicated Alaska Native women. In a lawsuit filed two years after the September 1999 incident, Susie Silook says once the assault on her was medically confirmed, the Anchorage Police Department failed to seriously investigate it. Silook is asking for monetary damages and an order directing the city to train its personnel to deal with such situations. Police spokesman Ron McGee said Wednesday that the department never comments on pending litigation. In court papers, the city's legal staff has asked that the suit be dismissed on a number of grounds, but has not given its version of events. Municipa…

title: Landmark land claims act still awaits resolution
summary: The vote was lopsided, to say the least. Of the 567 delegates to the Alaska Federation of Natives convention in Anchorage, 511 said yes to the Alaska Native Claims Settlement Act. The date was Saturday, Dec. 18, 1971. At 1 p.m. Alaska time, 45 minutes after the votes were tallied, President Nixon signed the bill into law, having been confident the AFN would approve it. He then phoned the delegates on a communications link set up hours before. "This is a milestone in Alaska's history and in the way our government deals with Native and Indian peoples," Nixon said. "It shows that institutions of government are responsive." ANCSA was the largest land claims settlement in American history. It provided for the federal government to give the state's indigenous peoples surface and mineral rights o…

title: Fishermen propose co-op to save expenses, maybe an industry
summary: Some fishermen in Alaska's embattled commercial salmon industry believe a proposal coming before the Alaska Board of Fisheries this week has potential to save livelihoods and salmon-dependent towns. But the idea is stirring a storm of controversy. It's called the Chignik proposal, and it involves turning a fleet of fierce fishing competitors into rubber-booted business partners. Every summer about 100 purse seine boats gather at Chignik, along the remote Alaska Peninsula, to net a million to 2 million sockeye salmon. In the late 1980s these fish fetched as much as $2.52 a pound at the dock for fishermen, but an avalanche of salmon from foreign fish farms has glutted markets and hammered the price of Alaska sockeye. In Chignik last summer, fishermen waged a bitter strike against the packing…

title: From gravel pit to The Terraces
summary: A developer has proposed turning another Anchorage gravel pit, the 75-acre hollow off Lake Otis Parkway south of O'Malley Road, into a planned community with more than 300 houses. It's the second major Anchorage gravel pit redevelopment announced in the past two months. A plan to build 650 houses on 252 acres of the old Sand Lake gravel pits is also up for city approval. These housing developments, to be built over several years, would take up two of the biggest remaining vacant parcels available for single-family homes in the Anchorage Bowl. They'll affect traffic, schools and neighborhood lifestyles. That's gotten the attention of residents in both areas. More broadly, the two gravel pit developments represent the first chance to put into play Anchorage's new long-range goals for neighbo…

title: Carrs replacing Cafe del Mundo, Kaladi with Starbucks
summary: Starbucks, the nation's largest specialty coffee chain, is extending its reach in Alaska through an alliance with Carrs Safeway, edging out Anchorage roasters Cafe del Mundo and Kaladi Bros. Seattle-based Starbucks recently replaced the two local brands at Carrs Safeway espresso bars inside the Muldoon and Eagle River grocery stores. Four Carrs Safeway locations in Anchorage and one in Juneau will ultimately serve Starbucks as the grocery chain tests customer preference, said Glenn Peterson, district manager. "We'll let the customers take a look at it," Peterson said. Carrs Safeway spokeswoman Cheri Myers described the switch to Starbucks as a trial, with customers having the final say. So far, everything indicates that Starbucks will be a hit, according to Myers. "Consumers have been anxi…

title: Senator calls for gas meet
summary: Fairbanks -- Sen. Frank Murkowski is calling for those interested in developing Alaska's North Slope gas reserves to a meet in Washington, D.C., early next year. Murkowski said he wants to get the gas producers and pipeline companies together in late January or early February. "If they don't want to meet together, then we'll extend the invitation individually," Murkowski said. A study group organized by Alaska's three major gas owners -- BP, Exxon Mobil and Phillips Petroleum -- has concluded that a pipeline is not economically feasible at this time. The final report hasn't been issued, however. But pipeline companies have been more optimistic about the project than the producers. A consortium of American and Canadian companies that had first proposed the gas line in the 1970s has agreed t…

title: Historic voyage retraced
summary: Vivian Mendenhall's first eco-tour netted plenty of opportunities to view colonies of sea birds in their native environments, but was equally interesting for its look into Native cultures and shipboard comraderie. Mendenhall, who has a doctorate in zoology and a 20-year career with the U.S. Fish and Wildlife under her belt, most of it in Alaska, was one of 24 nationally recognized scientists, artists and naturalists invited to lecture onboard the M/V Clipper Odyssey during a re-enactment of the Harriman Alaska Expedition. Thursday she will give a public talk and slide show at the Campbell Creek Science Center on the places, people and birds encountered during the trip. Industrialist Edward Harriman organized the 1899 expedition, assembling a crew of scientists, writers and artists willing …

title: Explorers excited about Donlin Creek deposit
summary: The estimated size of a gold deposit in Western Alaska has doubled, making it possibly the largest in the state's history, officials said Thursday. If the Donlin Creek deposit is developed, it could mean hundreds of year-round jobs for the rural area, said Greg Johnson, vice president of corporate development for NovaGold Resources Inc. The San Jose, Calif.-based company is a partner in Donlin and hopes to begin mining in three years, he said. Tests show Donlin Creek holds 23 million ounces of gold, Johnson said, nearly three times the size of the Fort Knox deposit near Fairbanks. At 3 grams of gold per ton, the Donlin deposit is three times as rich as Fort Knox too, Johnson said -- five times as rich in some places. It needs to be rich for development to begin, because the site is remote.…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Soldotna man charged in brutal attack RAPE, BEATING: Troopers arrest 20-year-old Justin Starkweather in Feb. 2 attack. By Jon Little Anchorage Daily News (Published: February 14, 2002) Alaska State Troopers arrested a Soldotna man Wednesday in connection with an attack on a 46-year-old Soldotna woman, who was raped and beaten almost to death earlier this month. Justin A. Starkweather, 20, was arrested without incident at his home at 3:20 p.m. and charged with attempted murder, first degree sexual assault and first degree burglary, troopers said. The attack took place at the woman's home, where she was found on the afternoon of Feb. 2. Starkweather was being held without bail at Wildwood Pre-Trial Facility in Kenai Wednesday night. The victim, who has not been identified, was taken to…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Field Notes (Published: February 10, 2002) TALKS Washburns to present Egan Center slide show Mount McKinley climbing legends Bradford and Barbara Washburn will be at the Egan Center on Monday evening for a reception and slide-show presentation on "Reminiscences: McKinley - Matterhorn - Everest." Bradford pioneered the now popular West Buttress route to the 20,320-foot summit of McKinley. Barbara was the first woman to reach the top of the mountain. Activities at the Egan Center start at 6 p.m. with the reception, exhibits and book and map signings. The slide show is at 7 p.m. followed by a question and answer session. Cost of admission to the slide show is $15. For more information, call The Maps Place at 563-6277. SKIING Ski patrol looking for new members The Anchorage Ski Patrol is…

title: From deer to falcons, board rules
summary: The Alaska Board of Game ruled this week on a broad range of issues, from allowing falconers to capture once-endangered American peregrine falcons to regulating airplanes or helicopters that fly too close to wildlife. The board voted down other proposals, including one that would have allowed hunters to use snowmachines to hunt wolves throughout much of the state and another allowing hunters to kill deer from boats in Southeast Alaska. The six-day Game Board meeting wraps up today at the Sheraton Anchorage Hotel. Falconry: The falconers didn't get all they wanted from the Game Board but did get one main request: permission to capture up to six peregrine falcons annually from nests south of the Alaska Range. The falconers train the birds to hunt waterfowl and other game birds. American pere…

title: Suspended dentist shows skills
summary: Dr. Leeland Cho was called to testify Wednesday for the first time at a hearing reviewing his suspended license, and he set up shop in the hearing room to show off his dentistry skills. "The state has said that you shouldn't practice dentistry, that you're incompetent," said Thomas Van Flein, Cho's attorney. Van Flein called Cho to the stand to try to show the state was wrong, asking Cho to demonstrate different dental procedures. Cho has been an Alaska dentist since 1991. Last month, the Alaska State Board of Dental Examiners voted to summarily suspend Cho's license after an expert witness concluded that Cho's practice was hazardous to his patients. David Stebing, a hearing officer for the state, is re-examining that decision. Van Flein acknowledged that the almost two-hour demonstration …

title: Flood covers Tanacross graveyard
summary: When people in Tanacross moved their village in the late 1970s, they left behind friends and family in the low-lying graveyard a stone's throw from a bend in the Tanana River. Now villagers are back and trying to save the old cemetery, submerged under the worst flooding in many years. Recent heavy rains and glacier melt on the river have combined to raise the river above its banks and, as of Thursday, the river was expected to rise an additional 6-8 inches. The flooding has filled the graveyard with knee-high water that threatens to expose graves and destroy the spirit houses that mark them, some more than 50 years old. "I wish we could send the water away," said village health aide Diane Titus, who has aunts and uncles buried in the cemetery among the 15-20 family gravesites. She describe…

title: Efforts focus on the mouth of Ship Creek
summary: Ship Creek likely won't be transformed into a free-flowing urban stream anytime soon, but people hoping to restore it to its natural condition are chipping away at obstructions that prevent fish from spawning upstream. The leading group behind the effort is the Anchorage Waterways Council, working mainly with the Alaska Department of Fish and Game, the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service and the U.S. Army Corps of Engineers. The idea is to someday remove the lower three of the creek's four dams to create 12 miles of undammed river for wild fish runs. Almost no salmon get past the second dam now. State biologists predict that if the dams are dismantled, fish will spawn farther upstream, as they once did naturally, and Ship Creek eventually could support tens of thousands more fish. In the short …

title: Mulder supports spending fund
summary: Juneau -- One of the most powerful legislators in the House, Finance Co-Chairman Eldon Mulder, is backing two bills to spend Alaska Permanent Fund earnings. One bill would use a portion of Permanent Fund earnings to refill a reserve account that is used each year to balance the state budget. Rep. Drew Scalzi, R-Homer, introduced that bill Friday. Mulder, an Anchorage Republican, has also signed on to a measure by Rep. Carl Moses, D-Unalaska, that would use Permanent Fund earnings to pay dividends to communities throughout the state. In exchange, the state would reduce the general fund money it sends to those communities. Mulder said the Moses bill is the most likely of the two measures to become part of a long-term fiscal plan adopted by the House this year. "I believe it is viable. I beli…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Hat Helen's lids cap Rondy show HIGHLIGHT: Senior's display is centerpiece of a heady collection. By S. Jane Szabo Anchorage Daily News (Published: February 16, 2002) "Hat Helen" hopped up from a desk at the Anchorage Senior Center when a visitor asked to see the hats amassed for the second annual Fur Rondy Hat Show. Helen Struz led the way into a room full of hats -- mostly her hats -- a couple of hours before the Hat Show on Wednesday. Her own collection could have made a show by itself. The day before, Struz had brought over her collection of hats representing 60 years and many places in the world. After the show she planned to let guests have fun trying on hats. Hatted even at midday in a cap crafted from cut-up Clorox bottles and red yarn, Struz gave a tour of her collection. Th…

title: The Anchorage Daily News (www.adn.com)
summary: SKIING, BOARDING Army eases rules for Alpenglow Ski Area With the fears of terrorism slowly easing, the Anchorage Ski Club reports the U.S. Army has decided to relax the restrictions on the road to the Alpenglow Ski Area in Arctic Valley. Skiers with Social Security and driver's license numbers on file are now allowed to drive up or down the road 10 a.m.-1:30 p.m. and 3:30-4:30 p.m. The Army will continue to check skiers in at the bottom of the road, but once checked in they will be allowed to drive up, according to the club. That's the good news. The bad news is that the shuttle bus that has taken skiers to the top of the mountain is about to disappear. The club says it is simply too costly to operate. For more details, call the hot line at 428-1208 or check out www.skialpenglow.com on th…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Brouhaha over beads Magazine article that mentions fishing technique illegal on Kenai raises anglers' ire By Craig Medred Anchorage Daily News (Published: February 17, 2002) A bead attached to a hook, left, is legal for rainbow trout angling on the Kenai River, while a bead fixed to a line is illegal. The illegal technique, touted in the February-March "Outdoor Life" magazine consists of pegging a bead to a line by jamming it with the end of a toothpick, which is then clipped off, thus securing the bead so it won't move. (Photo by Erik Hill / Anchorage Daily News) A Kenai River rainbow trout gives up the fight. title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Armed robber chased from store, outruns security guard By Lucas Wall Anchorage Daily News (Published: February 16, 2002) An armed man robbed the Brown Jug liquor store in Russian Jack on Friday evening and then had a gun pointed back at him. The incident began at 6:55 p.m. when the suspect entered the store at 1555 S. Bragaw St. wearing a black ski mask. He produced a small-caliber pistol and demanded money from the clerk, said Anchorage police officer Dan Thyen. The clerk handed over what Thyen described as a small amount of cash. O.C. Madden, Brown Jug's head of personnel and loss management, and security guard Jeff Patterson were at the store as the robbery occurred. Witnesses said Patterson drew a large handgun on the suspect and chased him out the front door. "All I could see wa…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Trial begins in death of trapper NENANA: Suspect faces murder, robbery and theft charges. The Associated Press Wire (Published: February 15, 2002) Fairbanks -- Testimony began Wednesday in the trial of one of two men charged with killing Nenana trapper John "Mackie" Burk. Mark Andrews, 26, of Nenana is being tried on a charge of first-degree murder as well as second-degree murder, robbery, burglary and theft. Burk, 66, was shot to death through his cabin door in January 2001. A second man, Darin Nathaniel of Minto, was charged in connection with the crime. Nathaniel, 19, reached a plea deal and was sentenced in July to 20 years in prison on a count of second-degree murder. In exchange for his plea, Nathaniel agreed to testify against Andrews. Andrews has waived his right to a jury tr…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Inside Alaska Business (Published: February 15, 2002) Three firms merge with Mikunda Three local accounting firms recently merged with Mikunda, Cottrell & Co.: Brunsvold & Co., Hanrahan & Co., and R. Meyer & Associates. Mikunda Cottrell said the firms' principals, Bruce Brunsvold, Michael Hanrahan and Rob Meyer, and their employees will join Mikunda Cottrell's staff. Anchorage-based Mikunda Cottrell has folded in six companies in the past three years. The mergers have expanded the accounting firm's areas of expertise to fill market gaps created as national firms have pulled out of Alaska to serve global clients, said David Cottrell, president and manager partner. Today's Pizza now open for lunch Today's Pizza, at 4608 Spenard Road in Anchorage, is now open for lunch as well as dinner…

title: New longliner rules aim to reduce seabird by-catch
summary: Anchorage -- Endangered short-tailed albatrosses and other seabirds with a fatal attraction to bait from longline fishing vessels got a safety net Sunday in regulations approved by a federal fisheries panel. The North Pacific Fishery Management Council adopted revised regulations requiring larger vessels in the hook-and-line groundfish fishery to fly bright-colored streamers from lines while setting gear. The streamer lines will create a bird-free corridor in which baited hooks can sink. The revised regulations, initially affecting larger vessels, are an issue the council has been wrestling with for several years. Thorn Smith, executive director of the North Pacific Longline Association in Seattle, said he supports the action. "We recognize that the goal is to reduce seabird by-catch and t…

title: Million teens to see science in Alaska
summary: Alaska will mug a frozen face for the camera during the next two weeks as nearly 1 million students worldwide watch live broadcasts of teenage scientists probing Portage Glacier and tackling biology at the Alaska SeaLife Center in Seward. The mega-event is the climax of the Jason Project, an interactive science program headed by Dr. Robert Ballard, the scientist who in 1985 discovered the Titanic wreckage. The project unfolds at a new venue each year. This time, students in 25,000 classrooms around the world are learning about Alaska's "Frozen Worlds." More than 100 crew members and researchers are in Alaska running experiments simultaneously at the two sites. Ice worms and glaciers, whales and seals and changing global weather patterns are part of a scientific buffet of live one-hour cybe…

title: Alaska, Canadian lawmakers meet
summary: Fairbanks -- A delegation of Alaska lawmakers is touring Western Canada to talk with Canadian government leaders about a proposed natural gas pipeline. The trip, which began Monday, comes on the heels of a consultant's warning that Alaskans should work more cooperatively with the Canadians or risk losing the proposed project altogether. Over the course of four days members of the Joint Committee on Natural Gas Pipelines will visit Whitehorse, Yukon Territory, Yellowknife, Northwest Territories, Edmonton, Alberta and Victoria, British Columbia. "Instead of guessing what each other is doing, we will sit down face to face," said Kasilof Republican Sen. John Torgerson, who chairs the legislative gas pipeline committee. Torgerson would like to see a joint U.S.-Canadian committee address natural…

title: Despite cutbacks, company stresses Alaska ambitions
summary: BP Exploration (Alaska) Inc. is sitting on 7 billion barrels of North Slope oil and gas and wants to recover as much as it can over the coming decades, president Steve Marshall said Friday. The oil is tucked in existing Slope fields and smaller ones nearby. But capturing the prize is no small task. It will take ingenuity and new technology to tap these deposits, many of which are viscous and don't flow to the surface easily, Marshall told peers at the Alaska Support Industry Alliance's annual conference at the Sheraton Anchorage Hotel. Marshall's speech was as much a company update as it was a reassurance to state leaders and industry peers, who have watched BP steadily shift from a frontier explorer in Alaska to a mature oil producer determined to milk long-established oil fields. BP Alas…

summary: | | | Well-groomed Hard-earned expertise in prepping city's ski trails reaches Olympic proportions By Natalie Phillips Anchorage Daily News (Published: February 3, 2002) By following groomers through Kincaid Park, a skier cashes in on the freshly groomed trail. (Photo by Erik Hill / Anchorage Daily News) Ben Powell and John Miles are framed by the tiller of a Pisten Bully trail grooming machine oustide their bunker headquarters at Kincaid Park. (Photo by Erik Hill / Anchorage Daily News) From the seat behind the controls of a snow cat, Ben Powell can look at the slashes in the snow left by a pair of skate skis and pretty much tell what kind of skier left them. title: Fish plants say banks doling out fewer chips
summary: A credit crunch is setting in on some Alaska salmon packers, jeopardizing their ability to operate their processing plants this summer. It's more evidence the state's commercial salmon industry is spoiling because of a worldwide salmon glut created in part by foreign salmon farms. Prices for salmon have sagged both to fishermen and to the packers who move the fish into wholesale markets, and now banks are more reluctant to make "pack loans" to processors, according to a Kodiak fishermen's association. Packers typically take out such loans to fund the coming season's operating expenses, including fish purchases, wages for plant workers, insurance, transportation and packaging supplies including tin cans. The United Salmon Association, which negotiates preseason price contracts between fishe…

title: Low prices, Lent yield hopes for big seafood sales
summary: Kodiak -- Nearly all seafood enjoys a surge of interest during Lent, Feb. 13 to March 31 this year, because of a Christian custom of abstaining from eating meat during the period. Retailers this year anticipate stronger-than-normal sales for shrimp and salmon because of lower prices. Lent also brings the year's highest demand for canned tuna, and sales in February and March have soared by 15 to 20 percent in the past decade. Sales of seafood products in the freezer cases -- such as fish sticks and portions -- are also stronger during Lent than at any other time of year, according to a report by WorldCatch. The only place where Lenten sales have lost some luster in recent years is at retail seafood counters. Farmed black cod goes to market. Totem Seafarms of Jervis Inlet, British Columbia, …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Two bills aimed at salving salmon industry's wounds FISH: Measure would make it easier to buy back permits. By Cathy Brown The Associated Press (Published: February 14, 2002) Juneau -- Bills aimed at increasing the efficiency of Alaska's beleaguered salmon industry are moving through the Legislature. The House Resources Committee on Wednesday approved a measure making it less cumbersome for the state to implement a limited entry permit buyback program. The committee is also looking at a bill that would let fishermen set up associations that could put together their own private buyback programs. Rep. Drew Scalzi, R-Homer, is sponsoring both bills. Both are aimed at reducing the number of nets in the water so the remaining fishermen stand a better chance of survival in a market flooded…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Denali KidCare may be trimmed HEALTH CARE: Bill would tighten income eligibility to cut costs. By Cathy Brown The Associated Press (Published: February 13, 2002) Juneau -- A proposal to drop the number of Alaskans eligible for a state health insurance program drew fire Tuesday from health care providers and those who would be affected by such cuts. Rep. John Coghill, R-North Pole, is sponsoring a bill to lower the income level at which families qualify for Denali KidCare -- a program that provides health insurance for kids and pregnant women. Currently, people can qualify if their income is 200 percent of the federal poverty level for Alaska. Coghill said that means a family of four earning $44,160 a year would qualify. That is too generous, he said, especially since the state is pro…

title: Yup'ik co-principal to handle language immersion
summary: Bethel -- For the first time since it opened in 1985, Ayaprun Elitnaurvik School, Bethel's Yup'ik immersion school, will have two principals -- one who speaks English and one who speaks Yup'ik. Principal Chris Meier and co-principal Agatha Panigkaq Shields joined forces to make the immersion program work this month. Meier will concentrate on the English components of the program, including administration duties; Shields will focus on the Yup'ik-language instruction and staff. Ayaprun Elitnaurvik School's advisory school board has always sought to hire a Yup'ik Eskimo principal fluent in the Native language. But qualified applicants have proven difficult to find, so this year the school created a co-principal position. Shields will be working toward achieving principal status through an int…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Downtown carnival is Rondy for many kids (Published: February 14, 2002) Real animals, as well as pelts, caught the crowd's attention at the Tanned Fur Auction at the Carnival Buttress Area on Sunday. Folks clustered around to pet and cuddle Siberian husky pups that a Wasilla family brought to try to sell. Children lingered at the auction to hold and pet the pups, then scampered off to ride the rides. The puppies were fun, but nothing like going around in circles really fast. To many kids, Rondy means the carnival that Golden Wheel Amusements runs on the corner of Third Avenue and E Street. There, the lucky win "Taz" stuffed animals and the unlucky run out of tickets and money. Sunday afternoon, the clouds lifted in time to serve carnival-goers mitten-must weather and an orange and pi…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Fishermen try to net funds for marketing TESTIMONY: House Finance Committee listens to arguments. By Ben Spiess Anchorage Daily News (Published: February 14, 2002) Juneau -- Alaska's floundering salmon industry made a call for help Wednesday, asking legislators for $12 million for marketing over the next five years. "This is a salmon crisis," said Homer fisherman Jamie Ross, speaking before the House Finance Committee. "We cannot exist without marketing." The request got sympathy from many members, but the plea may founder on Alaska's fiscal problem. The state projects a $1.1 billion revenue shortfall for next year's budget. More huge shortfalls are expected. The House is considering new taxes or other revenue. Propping up private industry with public money may be a tough sell. "I do…

title: House plan marks funds for tourism
summary: Juneau -- A bill to set aside $6 million to help Alaska's tourism industry market the state to vacationers is slated to be introduced in the state House. The funding would be given to the Alaska Travel Industry Association this year in an effort to lessen an expected drop in vacationers this summer due to the Sept. 11 terrorist attacks. House Finance Co-Chairman Eldon Mulder, R-Anchorage, said the measure would be filed as early as Friday. Budget leaders in the House and Senate said they have no plans to fund a similar effort for the state's salmon industry. Problems hobbling that industry -- such as a glut in prices from farm-raised fish -- are not likely to be fixed by an advertising campaign, Mulder said. "The emergency nature of it is not as clear," Mulder said. Senate Finance Co-Chair…

title: Confronting nature's threat
summary: Earthquakes and global warming pose serious natural hazards to Alaskans and their roads and buildings, experts told a workshop about risk held Thursday at the University of Alaska Anchorage. Some of the areas most threatened by earthquakes, for example, lie among the engines driving Alaska's economy -- the airfields and ports and pipelines of Southcentral Alaska. "There is a real need for science and engineering to mitigate the risk where we have our greatest economic infrastructure -- the Port of Anchorage, the airport and the Port of Valdez," University of Alaska Anchorage chancellor Lee Gorsuch told the engineers, scientists, planners and others attending. The trick? Since we can't stop the ground from shaking or the permafrost from melting, Alaskans can construct buildings and foundati…

title: Fairbanks firm gets gas line job
summary: Juneau -- A Fairbanks economic research firm was hired by a joint legislative committee to research North Slope natural gas issues. Northern Economic Research Associates was granted a $168,000 contract by the Joint Committee on Natural Gas Pipelines. The contract runs until June 30. "Natural gas represents an important new phase of Alaska's petroleum and petrochemical industry, and it is important that we have expert advice on how the state should proceed in pursuit of new opportunities," said Sen. John Torgerson, R-Kasilof, who chairs the joint committee. Northern will advise the committee on various proposals to construct natural gas pipelines to the Lower 48, he said. Alaska's oil industry is currently studying two proposals to ship about 37 trillion cubic feet of North Slope natural ga…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Poacher patrol Russian fishing fleets don't run wild, border guard says By Wesley Loy Anchorage Daily News (Published: February 9, 2002) Capt. Igor Rypalov, head of the Russian Federal Border Service, briefed the North Pacific Fishery Management Council this week on the activities of his agency, the equivalent of the U.S. Coast Guard. (Photo by Jim Lavrakas / Anchorage Daily News) Commercial fishing in the Russian Far East is not as lawless and poacher-plagued as some have portrayed it, according to a commander with Russia's coast guard. Capt. Igor Rypalov, a top commander for the Russian Federal Border Service in the Far East city of Petropavlovsk-Kamchatsky, said authorities there have a good fleet of patrol ships and planes, an elaborate system of setting catch limits, and rules r…

title: Fish farms pollution blasted
summary: The business of raising fish, shellfish and plants in pens has become "a gateway for exotic species" into U.S. waters and needs closer regulation and stricter standards, according to an essay in the current issue of the journal Science. More than 100 species are grown now in U.S. operations across all 50 states, with rapid expansion expected over the next five years, the three authors wrote in the Nov. 23 issue. "In the United States and abroad, aquaculture has led to introductions of unwanted seaweeds, fish, invertebrates, parasites, and pathogens," they said. "Accidental escapes and even purposeful releases create biological pollution' with irreversible and unpredictable ecological impacts." The authors cited escapes by Atlantic salmon from pens in Washington and Maine, and colonization …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Fishery council chief to run research panel PAUTSKE: New 19-member panel to coordinate studies in Pacific, Arctic, Bering waters. The Associated Press (Published: December 20, 2001) Kodiak -- Clarence Pautske, the executive director of the North Pacific Fishery Management Council, is leaving to run the newly established North Pacific Research Board. Pautske, who spent 21 years with the council, leaves Dec. 31 to take over the new Anchorage-based post. Congress established the 19-member board to coordinate marine research projects planned for the North Pacific, Bering Sea and Arctic Ocean. The panel is financed by the Environmental Improvement and Restoration Fund, which arose out of a legal dispute more than two decades ago. The 1979 Dinkum Sands lawsuit involved a dispute between th…

title: Native wisdom benefits biologists
summary: How do you follow a fast-swimming, deep-diving seal across polar ice? The initial ingredients are simple: Patience. A breathing hole. More patience. Follow that with a sheet of plywood, a secondhand transmitter, a dab of epoxy and at least one orbiting satellite. In a feat that married Inupiat seal-hunting know-how with Space Age gadgetry, villagers from Little Diomede Island worked with biologists last spring to capture and then track a ringed seal more than 400 miles through the frozen Chukchi Sea during the seal's annual northward migration. This unique collaboration between modern science and traditional knowledge marked the first time anyone has ever successfully followed a ringed seal through open sea ice by satellite tracking. It offered the first detailed glimpse of where the anima…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Oil companies face stiff opposition CONGRESS: Energy bill's amendment would force pipeline to run through Alaska. By Liz Ruskin Anchorage Daily News (Published: July 20, 2001) Washington -- A northern route may be cheaper, but if oil companies decide to build a pipeline to bring North Slope natural gas to the Lower 48, it will have to run south through Alaska, if Congress passes an amendment Rep. Don Young arranged to have slipped into a federal energy bill this week. The amendment, echoing a bill the Alaska Legislature passed this year, would bar the companies from building a gas line that follows an "over-the-top" route through the Beaufort Sea into northern Canada. A BP official in Anchorage told a legislative committee Wednesday the Beaufort route appears to be more cost efficien…

title: Failing marriage led to shootings
summary: Anchorage police have finished the criminal investigation of a murder-suicide in Eagle River on Feb. 1. John "Jed" Vanden Dries killed his wife, Catherine "Cam" Vanden Dries, in a premeditated act prompted by the couple's disintegrating marriage, investigators found. Jed Vanden Dries shot his wife, then himself, between 8 and 11 a.m. Feb. 1, police said. Police believe both shootings occurred in the parking lot along Eagle River Road where they found the car. Cam Vanden Dries, a teacher at William Tyson Elementary School, filed for divorce on Dec. 28. Friends told police that she restated her desire to end the marriage Jan. 31 during a rocky counseling session. About 7:40 a.m. Feb. 1, Jed Vanden Dries called his brother, Brian Vanden Dries, in Idaho and told him, " I've got the wills on th…

title: Hackney shifts his base to Washington
summary: Washington -- Longtime Anchorage political consultant Art Hackney is still working on Alaska campaigns, but he and his wife, April, have moved to the nation's capital to be part of George W. Bush's Washington. "It's arrogant to say, but I think we've sort of done it all in Alaska," Hackney said recently from his new office on Capitol Hill. "We worked on every campaign at every level, top to bottom." So, after serving as co-chairman for the Bush campaign in Alaska, Hackney went into partnership last year with Tom Edmonds, a Washington media consultant, for conservative causes. Edmonds Hackney and Associates has already hit a historic triple: The firm is working the 2002 campaigns of all three members of Alaska's delegation to Congress. Hackney's departure from Alaska leaves a void, said Chu…

title: Device may improve fish counting
summary: The same sonar technology that gives parents a close-up glimpse of their unborn children in the womb may be used to focus on Alaska salmon runs. Engineers at the University of Washington's Applied Physics Laboratory who developed a long-range ultrasound video system about a year ago will test it in mid-July on the Kenai River. State biologists long have used sonar to count fish swimming up the Kenai, but it has been, at times, impossible to differentiate between salmon species -- namely reds and kings -- or to calculate whether enough kings make it up river to spawn. The new device, called a surrogate camera, uses lenses and delivers images crisp enough to resemble video of salmon moving upstream. It shoots 96 sonar beams at high frequency and can discern objects as far as 30 meters away, …

title: DEC must revise spill rules
summary: State environmental regulators haven't been using a strong enough definition to enforce a law requiring companies that transport oil to keep the "best available technology" on hand to contain and clean up spills, the state Supreme Court said Friday. The ruling will require the Alaska Department of Environmental Conservation to either revise its regulations or ask the state Legislature to change the law. Commissioner Michele Brown late Friday said she and other agency officials were still digesting the opinion. In any event, the ruling will have little immediate practical effect, she said. DEC will continue to monitor and enforce spill response capabilities of companies that produce or ship oil while officials decide which course to take. Alaska's Oil Spill Pollution Control Act was passed …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Community Profile (Published: February 9, 2002) SAND POINT POPULATION: 952 (2001) LOCATION: On Humboldt Harbor on Popof Island, off the Alaska Peninsula, 570 air miles from Anchorage. DESCRIPTION: A self-sufficient, progressive community, with commercial fishing activities at the heart of the local culture. It is home to the largest fishing fleet in the Aleutian chain and several major fish-processing and storage operators. Nearly half the population is wholly or partly Alaska Native and participates in subsistence consumption of fish and caribou. A large transient population arrives seasonally for fishing and cannery work. The one school is attended by 113 students. Two exploratory mining operations are currently under way nearby. title: Unalaska wants dancers to put on their clothes
summary: Unalaska -- Less than a month after the opening of a strip club here, the Unalaska City Council has taken the first step toward an ordinance that would bar public nudity. If it is approved, the law will ban fully nude performances and will require dancers to wear pasties on their breasts. The Mermaids nonalcoholic club opened in downtown Unalaska on Jan. 11. On Tuesday, the council voted unanimously to move forward on the ordinance banning public nudity, citing public complaints and studies that link such businesses to crime. Except for some discussion about reworking an awkward passage, council members viewed the ordinance without comment. Under the ordinance, a violation of the law would result in a $300 fine. Repeated violations would eventually bring a fine of as much as $1,000 and up …

title: Interior official seeks advisory council review
summary: The citizen councils that help direct subsistence management on federal lands in Alaska may be weighted with rural subsistence users at the expense of other hunters and fishermen, according to the U.S. Department of the Interior. Deputy Interior Secretary Steven Griles told Federal Subsistence Board Chairman Mitch Demientieff in a letter Jan. 8 that Alaska's 10 regional advisory councils need to include urban residents, sportsmen and commercial fishermen in addition to rural subsistence users. "We need to see that all of these groups and any others you may identify who are directly affected by the Subsistence Management Program are represented," Griles wrote. The letter did not accuse the councils of bias. But in a clear directive from Washington, D.C., it instructs the Federal Subsistence…

title: Coast Guard heightens security role in Sound
summary: Fairbanks -- Fishermen and tourists will see armed Coast Guard boats patrolling the waters of Prince William Sound this summer. Following the Sept. 11 terrorist attacks, the Coast Guard established security zones in the Port of Valdez and Valdez Narrows to protect the Alyeska Pipeline Service Co.'s Valdez Marine Terminal and oil tankers entering or leaving Valdez. "Right now pretty much the whole port is closed to everything, but that's going to change," said Lt. Keith Ropella, chief of traffic service for the Coast Guard in Valdez. "We were able to do that because of the very light winter traffic." In the summer, however, Valdez is one of Alaska's hottest fishing and tourist destinations. Both fishermen and tourists flock to Valdez to catch salmon and tour Prince William Sound by boat. Wh…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Natives gather for convention FOCUS: Subsistence and racism are likely to be hot topics at 36th annual event. By Zaz Hollander Anchorage Daily News (Published: October 21, 2001) The largest gathering of Alaska Natives in the state gets under way Monday with a focus on subsistence and racism and the air of a family reunion. About 3,000 Eskimos, Indians and Aleuts are expected at the annual convention of the Alaska Federation of Natives, the 36th since 1966. This is no stodgy dentists' convention: Elders get seats of honor in the front of the room, kids are welcome, and a Native arts and crafts sale bustles downstairs. The week's events begin when the Elders and Youth Conference convenes on Monday, and continues until the AFN delegates wrap up action on a series of resolutions Saturday…

title: Ballot system to aid blind
summary: Like thousands of other blind Alaskans, Sandy Sanderson lost the ability to vote privately when he lost his eyesight. For more than 25 years, he has had to state his election picks aloud to someone in the voting booth, who reads the ballot to him and marks his choices. Now, the mess of the 2000 presidential election is opening up an opportunity for Sanderson and other blind and disabled Alaskans to vote without help. The state Division of Elections is considering buying a small number of electronic voting machines that would eliminate the need for a paper ballot. Visually impaired voters would listen to their choices on a headset and navigate the ballot through electronic buttons. People who can see wouldn't need the headset and would vote either by touching a screen or by using buttons co…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Democrats kill Fairbanks debate SUBSISTENCE Taylor proposal called a campaign stunt. By Mike Chambers The Associated Press (Published: February 9, 2002) Juneau -- A move to take the subsistence debate to Fairbanks, where resistance to a rural priority has traditionally been strong, was turned away by Senate Democrats on Friday. Senate Minority Leader Johnny Ellis called the plan a campaign stunt by Sen. Robin Taylor, who is running for lieutenant governor. "The whole thing has definite political and electoral overtones," said Ellis, a Democrat from Anchorage. "I think the Republicans really ought to reflect on whether that is a wise use of public funds." Taylor is a Republican from Wrangell who chairs the state Senate Judiciary Committee. title: Harry Potter books inspire new love for literature
summary: Alaina Alderson has learned books do more than entertain -- sometimes they jump right off the page like magic and change your life. The 12-year-old was introduced to "Harry Potter and the Sorcerer's Stone" when her grandmother dropped a copy on her bed and asked her to read a few pages. "I started reading and I was just captured," Alaina said. She's not alone. The popularity of J.K. Rowling's Harry Potter series has reached epic proportions, selling 3 million copies before the fourth in the seven-book series was released. The movie "Harry Potter and the Sorcerer's Stone" has topped $855 million in ticket sales worldwide. Rowling's winning formula -- every chapter ends in a cliff-hanger -- appeals to adults and children alike. The tale of the magically gifted orphan and his friends at Hogwa…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Meeting tackles domestic violence MINORITIES: Groups are more likely to be victims and offenders. By Lisa Demer Anchorage Daily News (Published: October 23, 2001) City leaders are tackling a sensitive social issue head-on: the high rates of domestic violence among blacks and Alaska Natives. "We are identifying that as an issue, because we want to do something different about it," said Carrie Longoria, manager of the municipality's Safe City program. "We're not just saying, Isn't it a shame?' " Groundwork for the effort is being laid this week at a "Cultural Competency" conference. Counselors, probation officers, police, prosecutors and others are examining their own beliefs -- about discrimination, ethnic differences, color -- as they learn better techniques for working with diverse …

title: Injured police chief can't sue for negligence
summary: The Alaska Supreme Court followed the lead of other states in a ruling Friday that said injured police officers and firefighters can't sue people whose negligence caused them to be called out in the first place. Dealing with other people's negligence is part of the job for public safety workers, the court said, and they receive "compensation and benefits for the risks inherent in such responses." The issue arose when Dillingham Police Chief Brent Moody sued a local company whose stolen fuel truck had rammed into a vehicle he was in, causing permanent injuries. The truck was stolen by Joseph Coolidge, who was drunk, after an employee of Delta Western Inc. left a loaded fuel truck in a driveway with the door unlocked and the keys in the ignition. Moody responded to a call that the truck was …

title: Satellite imagery may help in beetle battle
summary: Kenai -- Satellite imagery and photos shot from high-flying aircraft soon could be deployed in the battle against the spruce bark beetle infestation that has devastated forests on the Kenai Peninsula. The Kenai Peninsula Borough Assembly this week considered an ordinance to appropriate $271,000 in federal money to begin monitoring a 60-square-mile forested test area between Kenai and Nikiski bordering both sides of the Kenai Spur Highway. The area is heavy in mature trees, the kind favored by the beetles. Satellite photos will not stop the tiny insects from infecting trees, but analysis of the thermal imagery could help foresters and entomologists detect newly infected trees -- so-called stressed trees -- long before their needles turn brown, according to Bob Bright, borough planning direc…

title: Panel OKs $1.1 million for ANWR
summary: Juneau -- A House panel on Tuesday approved spending $1.1 million more this year to push opening the Arctic National Wildlife Refuge to oil drilling, despite hearing opposition from environmentalists. The House Finance Committee bill would give $1 million to the nonprofit lobbying group Arctic Power and $100,000 to the North Slope community of Kaktovik. The spending would bring to $3 million the amount of state money provided to Arctic Power in the fiscal year that ends June 30, said Kim Duke, Arctic Power executive director. Duke said the group plans to spend the extra $1 million on a "very intense" three-month campaign. The Kaktovik funding is intended to help the village prepare a video in support of opening ANWR and help defray its expenses in handling the influx of visitors who pass t…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Seward receives state's first 'TsunamiReady' designation SAFETY: City is one of three in the nation to be honored for emergency readiness. By Peter Porco Anchorage Daily News (Published: January 19, 2002) If you were standing somewhere in Seward while a tsunami, or huge sea wave, were bearing down on the town, there's almost no way you wouldn't hear about it. Therefore, you would have time at least to make a move toward higher, safer ground. Seward, which just won the honor of being designated Alaska's first "TsunamiReady" community, has multiple means of getting the word out. Emergency personnel, for example, would sound a siren throughout the town, warning specifically of a coming tsunami. They also would engage a computerized phone-calling system, use the public-address speakers o…

title: Smoking ban a hit, poll shows
summary: Anchorage's restaurant smoking ban isn't just popular, it has actually encouraged people to eat out, a poll released Thursday shows. The poll, conducted by Ivan Moore this month for the Alaska Native Health Board, comes near the end of the first year of the smoking ban and just days before the city is due to get another report weighing its effectiveness. Of those polled, nearly 80 percent support the ban and almost 22 percent go to some restaurants more because they are smoke-free. Only 6.8 percent said the reverse: that they stopped going to some restaurants as much because they went smoke-free. "The numbers are extremely strong; they show overwhelmingly that this is a good thing," said Annette Marley, manager of the Alaska Native Health Board's Trampling Tobacco Project. The city should …

title: Lectures to examine Alaska wonders
summary: In Alaska, science is a living document. Whether it's the celestial splash of the aurora, the earth-ripping violence of volcanoes, the grip of glaciers or the patient progress of bears and caribou, in many parts of the state it is impossible to avoid the evidence of powerful forces at work, forces obeying the laws of science. Those laws are the subject of a six-week series of free public lectures, called Science for Alaska, that begin next week in Anchorage, Fairbanks and Juneau. The topics include the aurora, volcanoes, glaciers, seals, bears, caribou and "other Alaska wonders," according to the coordinator of the series, the Geophysical Institute at the University of Alaska Fairbanks. The multimedia presentations will involve slide shows and videos that should appeal to every age group, …

title: Letters to the Editor
